You've been learning about the principle of unity, the idea that as a persuader, everything becomes easier if it's possible to arrange for your audience to see you as one of them. Because inside the boundaries of the concept of we, all influence becomes easier. People are more willing to believe you, more willing to cooperate with you, more willing to compensate you, more willing to say yes to your requests. A powerful factor that leads people to see you as one of them is shared locality. People see those who share their living space, their neighborhood, their community as one of them, as part of their identity. So people say, I'm a New Yorker, I'm a Minnesotan, I'm a Londoner. Think about the connection you felt the last time you met a complete stranger while traveling and discovered you were from the same hometown. These kinds of local identities carry the unifying power of place. Let me give you one of the most graphic examples I know. It's a difficult one to describe because it was so toxic. During the years of Nazi ascendancy in Europe, many people were sent to concentration camps, Gypsies, political dissidents, but for the most part they were Jewish residents of the towns and cities in Europe. Some of the camps were work camps. In these work camps, when any one prisoner violated a rule, it wasn't uncommon for everyone to be lined up and as a punishment for the violation, a guard would go down the line counting each person until he came to the 10th, who he would shoot dead. Then he would count to the next 10th person and shoot him or her dead and the next 10th person and the next until he got to the end of the line. There's a sociologist named Ronald Cohen who found an informant who was a guard at one of the camps, not the one who did the shooting, but one who often observed the process. And one day he witnessed something that puzzled him deeply. A cold blooded guard was doing his deadly duty, going down the line, executing every 10th prisoner until coming to one such 10th man, whereupon he stopped for a second, made a quarter turn and shot the 11th prisoner, after which he continued down the line, once again executing every 10th person. Cohen's puzzled informant asked the murderous guard afterward, I notice you didn't shoot the 10th man about halfway through. Why was that? Was he an especially good worker? Someone you thought would be useful to the camp in some way because of some skill he had, maybe? The guard laughed and said, I don't care about any of that. It was because I recognized him. We grew up in the same town. That was enough to cause this brutal assassin to become a benefactor, but a very narrow benefactor, only to someone who could claim unity of place with him. OK, I'm going to move away now from this story to say something about the beneficial impact of this form of unity for you. Always look for and mention any commonality of place that you truly share with the person you're trying to move in your direction. It has a remarkable force.
